title: U.S. forces strike Iranian Revolutionary Guard positions in Syria after drone attack on base
topicContext: EUR/USD is the most-traded currency pair in the world. It is sensitive to relative monetary policy between the European Central Bank and the Federal Reserve, eurozone vs U.S. growth differentials, risk-on/risk-off flows, and geopolitical events that affect the dollar's safe-haven status. The pair typically falls (USD strengthens) during global risk-off episodes and rises (USD weakens) during periods of stability and growth optimism. Major support levels in the current range: 1.0820, 1.0780, 1.0720. Resistance: 1.0920, 1.0980.
body:
U.S. military forces conducted airstrikes on positions belonging to Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps in eastern Syria early on Monday, the Pentagon said, in retaliation for a drone attack on a U.S. base in northeast Syria that killed three American service members and wounded 25 others over the weekend.

The strikes hit multiple sites used by Iran-backed militias near the towns of Mayadin and Abu Kamal, according to a Pentagon statement. U.S. defense officials said the operation was 'precise and proportional' and was intended to degrade the militias' ability to launch further attacks on U.S. personnel in the region.

Iranian state media confirmed the strikes and said the country would 'respond at a time and place of our choosing.' Iran's foreign minister called the U.S. action 'a flagrant violation of Syrian sovereignty' and said it would escalate tensions across the region.

Oil prices spiked on the news, with Brent crude rising nearly 4% in early European trading to above $92 per barrel, the highest level since November. Traders cited concerns about potential disruption to shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20% of global oil supplies transit.

Gold also rallied, climbing to $2,365 per ounce, near a record high, as investors moved into safe-haven assets. The Japanese yen and Swiss franc both strengthened against the dollar, while emerging-market currencies came under pressure.

U.S. officials told Reuters that the administration is considering further military options if Iran or its proxies launch additional attacks on American forces. A senior administration official said the White House is in close contact with Israel, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE about the regional implications.

Market analysts said the situation poses fresh upside risk to oil prices and downside risk to global growth, particularly for energy-importing economies in Europe and Asia. 'This is exactly the kind of geopolitical shock that markets have been pricing as a tail risk,' said one London-based strategist. 'Whether it stays contained or escalates into something broader will depend on what Iran decides to do next.'

The European Central Bank declined to comment on the market impact. The Federal Reserve, which meets later this month, is expected by markets to maintain its current policy stance, though analysts noted that a sustained spike in oil prices could complicate the inflation outlook on both sides of the Atlantic.
